column1: What is the definition of the derivative of \(f\) at \(a\)?
column2:
A function \(f\) is differentiable at \(a\) if the limit
\[f'(a)=\lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(a+h)-f(a)}{h}\]
exists as a finite real number.

column1: What does it mean for \(f\) to be continuous at \(a\)?
column2:
A function \(f\) is continuous at \(a\) if
\[\lim_{x\to a} f(x)=f(a).\]
Equivalently: for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta>0\) such that if \(|x-a|<\delta\), then \(|f(x)-f(a)|<\varepsilon\).

column1: What is the \(\varepsilon\)-\(\delta\) definition of \(\lim_{x\to a} f(x)=L\)?
column2:
\[\lim_{x\to a} f(x)=L\]
means: for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta>0\) such that if \(0<|x-a|<\delta\), then \(|f(x)-L|<\varepsilon\).

column1: What is the definition of convergence of a sequence \((a_n)\) to \(L\)?
column2:
The sequence \((a_n)\) converges to \(L\) if for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists \(N\in\mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n\ge N\),
\[|a_n-L|<\varepsilon.\]

column1: What is the definition of uniform continuity of \(f\) on a set \(A\)?
column2:
A function \(f:A\to\mathbb{R}\) is uniformly continuous on \(A\) if for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta>0\) such that for all \(x,y\in A\), if \(|x-y|<\delta\), then
\[|f(x)-f(y)|<\varepsilon.\]
The key point is that \(\delta\) depends only on \(\varepsilon\), not on the point.

If \(f\) is continuous on the closed interval \([a,b]\), and \(N\) is any number between \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\), then there exists \(c\in[a,b]\) such that
\[f(c)=N.\]

If \(f\) is continuous on the closed interval \([a,b]\), then \(f\) attains both an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on \([a,b]\). That is, there exist \(x_{\min},x_{\max}\in[a,b]\) such that
\[f(x_{\min})\le f(x)\le f(x_{\max})\quad\text{for all }x\in[a,b].\]

If \(f\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), differentiable on \((a,b)\), and \(f(a)=f(b)\), then there exists \(c\in(a,b)\) such that
\[f'(c)=0.\]

If \(f\) is continuous on \([a,b]\) and differentiable on \((a,b)\), then there exists \(c\in(a,b)\) such that
\[f'(c)=\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}.\]

column1: What does differentiable at \(a\) imply about continuity at \(a\)?
column2: If \(f\) is differentiable at \(a\), then \(f\) is continuous at \(a\). Differentiability is strictly stronger than continuity.

column1: What is the definition of a Cauchy sequence?
column2:
A sequence \((a_n)\) is Cauchy if for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists \(N\in\mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(m,n\ge N\),
\[|a_n-a_m|<\varepsilon.\]

column1: What is the completeness property of \(\mathbb{R}\)?
column2: Every Cauchy sequence of real numbers converges to a real number. Equivalently, every nonempty subset of \(\mathbb{R}\) that is bounded above has a least upper bound (supremum).
